trap: Builder for simple 2-3 field objects -- YAGNI
description: Don't create a Builder when the object has 2-3 required fields and no optionals. A simple constructor is clearer. Builder is ceremony without value here.
fix: Ask: 4+ fields? Optional fields? Cross-field validation? Computed fields? If NO to all, skip Builder.

trap: build() that just copies fields -- no validation
description: If build() just does 'return new Product(this)' without any checks, you've written 50 lines of Builder boilerplate for zero safety benefit. You just have a complicated setter wrapper.
fix: build() MUST validate required fields, cross-field constraints, and computed values. That's the whole point of centralizing construction.

trap: Product with setters -- defeats immutability guarantee
description: If the Product has setters, anyone can modify it after build(). The Builder's promise of 'born perfect, forever final' is broken. Why use Builder if the product is mutable?
fix: All Product fields must be final. No setters on the Product. Only getters. The Builder is the ONLY way to set values.

trap: Sharing mutable collections between Builder and Product
description: If the Product's constructor copies the Builder's list by reference, mutating the Builder's list after build() corrupts the 'immutable' product.
fix: Always use List.copyOf(), Map.copyOf(), or Collections.unmodifiable*() in the Product constructor. Final reference != immutable contents.

trap: One Builder creating multiple unrelated product types
description: A Builder that builds meals AND drinks AND desserts based on flags is a God Builder. It violates SRP and has terrible type safety.
fix: One Builder per Product type. If build() returns Object or uses instanceof, you've gone wrong.
